questions.stem: In an OOPs interview, distinguish a class from an object. Which response is most accurate?
options: A class is a blueprint/type; an object is a runtime instance of that type
A class is always mutable; an object is always a static method
Objects define fields; classes can only define interfaces
Classes exist only at runtime; objects exist only in source files
questions.explanation: Classes describe structure and behavior; objects are concrete instances in memory.

questions.stem: Encapsulation — what core idea should you state in one crisp interview line?
options: Inherit every method from Object without overrides
Bundle state with behavior and hide internals behind a controlled interface
Make all fields public so serializers can access them
Replace classes with global functions exclusively
questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides representation and exposes a stable API for interaction.

questions.stem: Abstraction versus encapsulation — how do interviewers often separate them?
options: Abstraction only applies to interfaces; encapsulation only to structs
They are exact synonyms with no useful distinction
Abstraction highlights what; encapsulation hides how / protects state
Encapsulation deletes unused methods; abstraction adds logging
questions.explanation: Abstraction models essential concepts; encapsulation controls access to details.

questions.stem: Inheritance in OOP: what relationship does it primarily express between two types?
options: A runtime cast that always succeeds between any types
Two unrelated classes sharing a package name only
Composition where the child owns the parent exclusively
A subtype reuses and specializes a base type's contract and implementation
questions.explanation: Inheritance creates an is-a hierarchy for shared behavior and polymorphism.

questions.stem: Polymorphism — what is the simplest correct definition you should give in an interview?
options: Same message/interface, different concrete behavior depending on the receiver
Copying fields from one object into another by reflection
Compiling the same source file twice with different flags
Making every method static and final
questions.explanation: Callers depend on a common type; runtime (or generics) selects the right behavior.

questions.stem: Method overloading versus overriding — which distinction is correct for interviews?
options: Overriding changes parameter lists only; overloading replaces parent bodies only
Overloading: same name, different signatures; overriding: redefine inherited method
They are identical terms in Java and C++
Overloading requires inheritance; overriding forbids it
questions.explanation: Overload = compile-time signature variants; override = subtype replaces inherited method.

questions.stem: Interface versus abstract class (Java-leaning): which guidance is most accurate?
options: You can extend many abstract classes but implement only one interface
Abstract classes cannot declare methods; interfaces must have fields only
Interfaces define contracts (often multi); abstract classes share partial implementation
Interfaces are instantiated directly; abstract classes never have subclasses
questions.explanation: Prefer interfaces for capability contracts; abstract classes for shared base code.

questions.stem: Composition versus inheritance — when do senior engineers typically prefer composition?
options: When equals and hashCode must be identical for all types
When you must share protected fields across an entire hierarchy
When polymorphism is banned by the language
When you need has-a reuse without tight is-a coupling and fragile base classes
questions.explanation: Composition delegates to collaborators; it avoids deep brittle inheritance trees.

questions.stem: Access modifiers (public/protected/private): what does private primarily protect?
options: Members visible only inside the declaring class (typical Java/C# rule)
Members visible to every package in the JVM automatically
Members that subclasses must override
Members that serializers must always include
questions.explanation: private restricts access to the enclosing class, enforcing encapsulation.

questions.stem: Constructor responsibility: what should a well-designed constructor guarantee?
options: All static methods of the class are invoked once
The new object starts in a valid, usable state (invariants established)
The object is registered as a singleton globally
equals returns true when compared to null
questions.explanation: Construction should establish invariants so methods can assume a valid object.

questions.stem: Immutable object benefits — which interview talking point is the strongest and clearest?
options: Immutable objects cannot override equals ever
Immutability forbids implementing interfaces
Safer sharing across threads and simpler reasoning; great as map keys if hash stable
Immutability always uses less memory than mutables
questions.explanation: Immutable values avoid aliasing bugs and are naturally concurrency-friendly.

questions.stem: Dynamic dispatch (virtual calls): what happens at a Shape.draw() call on a Circle?
options: Dispatch picks a random subclass method for load balancing
The compiler always inlines Shape.draw and ignores Circle
Only static methods participate in dynamic dispatch
The runtime selects Circle.draw based on the actual object type
questions.explanation: Virtual dispatch uses the receiver's runtime type to pick the override.

questions.stem: SOLID — how do you state the Single Responsibility Principle in one clear interview line?
options: A class should have one reason to change — one cohesive responsibility
A class must have exactly one method and one field
Every class must inherit from a single God object
Responsibilities should be duplicated across layers freely
questions.explanation: SRP: cohesion around one axis of change, not a literal one-method rule.

questions.stem: Open/Closed Principle — which phrasing is the interview-ready definition of OCP?
options: Open source only; closed for unit tests
Open for extension, closed for modification of existing proven code
Never add new classes; only edit existing ones
Seal every class and forbid interfaces
questions.explanation: Extend via new types/strategies rather than constantly rewriting stable modules.

questions.stem: Liskov Substitution Principle: what must remain true for subtypes of a base type?
options: Subtypes must hide all parent methods as private
Subtypes may strengthen preconditions and weaken postconditions freely
Subtype instances must be usable wherever the base type is expected without breaking clients
Subtypes cannot override any method from the parent
questions.explanation: LSP: subtypes honor the base contract so polymorphic clients keep working.

questions.stem: Interface Segregation Principle — what design problem does ISP primarily fight?
options: Making value objects immutable
Having too many small focused interfaces
Using composition instead of inheritance
Fat interfaces forcing clients to depend on methods they do not use
questions.explanation: ISP: split broad interfaces so clients depend only on what they need.

questions.stem: Dependency Inversion Principle — what is the core idea you should recite in interviews?
options: High-level modules depend on abstractions, not concrete low-level details
Every class must new up its dependencies internally
UI layers should import database drivers directly
Abstractions must depend on concrete framework classes
questions.explanation: Both high- and low-level code depend on shared abstractions (often injected).

questions.stem: Java equals/hashCode contract — what essential rule must you be able to recite?
options: equals can ignore hashCode with no rules
Equal objects must share hashCode; unequal may collide; mutate keys carefully
hashCode must be unique for every distinct object
hashCode may change freely after insert into a HashMap
questions.explanation: Consistency between equals and hashCode is mandatory for hash-based collections.

questions.stem: static method versus instance method — what is the key behavioral difference to name?
options: Instance methods cannot access private fields
Static methods always participate in dynamic dispatch on this
Static binds to the class; no receiver polymorphism via overriding in the usual sense
Static methods require a non-null this reference
questions.explanation: static is class-level; instance methods dispatch on the object identity/type.

questions.stem: What is a pure abstract type or interface especially good for in public API design?
options: Guaranteeing O(1) performance for all methods
Storing mutable global configuration for the whole process
Replacing the need for any concrete classes
Defining a capability contract so implementations can vary behind it
questions.explanation: Interfaces decouple callers from concrete implementations and enable mocking/testing.

questions.stem: Fragile base class problem: why can changing a parent method break distant subclasses?
options: Subclasses couple to inherited behavior and overrides interact subtly with parent changes
Inheritance forbids method overriding by language rule
Parent classes cannot have protected members
Composition always creates the same fragility
questions.explanation: Deep inheritance makes subclasses brittle when base implementation evolves.

questions.stem: Template Method pattern (behavioral inheritance): what does the base class fix?
options: The concrete SQL dialect used by every repository
An algorithm skeleton with overridable hook steps in subclasses
That all methods must be static factories
A guarantee that equals ignores identity
questions.explanation: Base method orchestrates steps; subclasses supply varying primitive operations.

questions.stem: Covariance of return types on overrides (Java): which statement is true?
options: Return types cannot change in any override ever
Overrides must always return Object regardless of parent
An override may return a more specific subtype than the parent method
Covariant returns only apply to static methods
questions.explanation: Java allows covariant return types so overrides can narrow the result type.

questions.stem: Why is implementing equals based only on mutable fields dangerous for HashSet membership?
options: Mutable fields make hashCode final automatically
HashSet forbids custom equals entirely
equals is never called during HashSet.contains
Mutating fields after insert can lose the object in the wrong bucket
questions.explanation: If equals/hashCode inputs change, the collection cannot find the entry later.

questions.stem: Composition root / dependency injection: what problem does constructor injection solve?
options: Makes required collaborators explicit and avoids hidden service locators
Eliminates the need for interfaces in the codebase
Forces every class to be a singleton
Allows circular dependencies without any design cost
questions.explanation: Constructor injection surfaces dependencies and supports DIP/testability.

questions.stem: Marker interface versus annotation (Java history): what is the modern preference?
options: Marker interfaces are illegal after Java 8
Prefer annotations for metadata; marker interfaces still used for type constraints
Annotations replace the type system completely
Neither can influence instanceof checks
questions.explanation: Annotations carry metadata; marker interfaces still give compile-time type tokens.

questions.stem: Law of Demeter / tell-don't-ask: which code smell violates it most clearly?
options: Injecting a ShippingService into OrderService
order.ship() asking the order to perform shipping
order.getCustomer().getAddress().getZip() chaining through strangers
Using a local variable for an intermediate calculation
questions.explanation: Long getter chains couple you to alien object graphs; prefer asking collaborators.

questions.stem: Value object versus entity (DDD-flavored OOP): what is the distinguishing trait?
options: Entities cannot implement equals at all
Entities are always immutable; value objects are always mutable
Value objects must have database surrogate keys
Value objects compare by attributes; entities have identity over time
questions.explanation: Entities track identity; value objects are interchangeable when attributes match.

questions.stem: Defensive copy on getters that return internal lists — why bother with the extra copy?
options: Callers mutating the returned list would break the owner's encapsulation/invariants
Defensive copies make equals faster always
Java forbids returning List types without copying
It is required for every primitive int getter
questions.explanation: Exposing live mutable internals lets outsiders violate encapsulation.

questions.stem: final class or sealed hierarchy — what design goal are you usually trying to achieve?
options: Force every subclass to override equals and hashCode
Restrict extension so behavior and security assumptions stay closed
Disable polymorphism for interfaces
Make garbage collection run after every method
questions.explanation: Sealing/final limits who can extend types, preserving invariants and exhaustive switches.

questions.stem: Mixin / default interface methods: what benefit and risk should you mention together?
options: Default methods cannot be overridden by implementors
They replace the need for any concrete classes safely forever
Share behavior across types; risk diamond conflicts and unclear state ownership
They make all implementing classes immutable automatically
questions.explanation: Defaults help evolution of interfaces but complicate multiple-inheritance conflicts.

questions.stem: Covariance vs contravariance intuition for generics (PECS): which is right?
options: Contravariance only applies to primitive arrays
All generic types are invariant and PECS never applies
List<Dog> is always a List<Animal> for writes and reads
Producers extend (out/? extends); consumers super (in/? super) for safe variance
questions.explanation: PECS guides wildcards so reads/writes stay type-safe under variance.

questions.stem: Tell-don't-ask applied to a BankAccount: which API is more object-oriented?
options: account.withdraw(amount) encapsulating balance checks inside
if (account.getBalance() >= amount) account.setBalance(balance - amount)
Reading balance via reflection then mutating the field
Making balance a public field updated by every caller
questions.explanation: Tell the object to withdraw; don't pull state out and decide externally.

questions.stem: Copy constructor versus clone() — what preference do most interviewers expect to hear?
options: clone() is always deep and never throws
Explicit copy constructors/factories are clearer and less error-prone than Object.clone
Copy constructors cannot copy final fields
clone() is required by the equals contract
questions.explanation: clone is awkward (Cloneable, checked exceptions); explicit copies are clearer.

questions.stem: Anemic domain model criticism: what is missing when entities are data bags?
options: ORM tools cannot map anemic objects
Too many methods on entities violating SRP always
Behavior and invariants live outside in services, weakening encapsulation
Anemic models forbid DTOs by definition
questions.explanation: Rich models keep business rules with the data they protect.

questions.stem: Factory Method versus simple constructor: when is a factory justified?
options: When you want to bypass access modifiers illegally
Whenever a class has more than one field
Only for immutable strings in the JDK
When creation logic varies, caches, or returns subclass instances polymorphically
questions.explanation: Factories encapsulate non-trivial or polymorphic construction decisions.

questions.stem: Symmetric equals: why must a.equals(b) imply b.equals(a) for a correct contract?
options: Collections and algorithms assume equivalence relations; asymmetry breaks them
Java compilers enforce symmetry with a special keyword
hashCode ignores equals when symmetry fails
Asymmetry is required for inheritance hierarchies
questions.explanation: equals must be an equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric, transitive.

questions.stem: Prefer interfaces for return types of public APIs — why is that considered good design?
options: Interfaces are faster than concrete classes at runtime always
Callers depend on contracts; you can swap ArrayList for LinkedList later
Concrete returns are illegal in Java
Interfaces automatically deep-copy returned collections
questions.explanation: Program to an interface so implementations can evolve without breaking callers.

questions.stem: Object identity (==) versus equality (equals) in Java — interview contrast?
options: equals cannot be overridden; only == is customizable
== always compares field-by-field contents for objects
== compares references (for objects); equals compares logical value when overridden
They are identical for all reference types
questions.explanation: == is reference identity; equals is value semantics when properly implemented.

questions.stem: Mixin composition via delegation: how do you simulate multiple inheritance safely?
options: Make all methods static and call them with class names
Extend two concrete classes in languages that forbid it
Copy-paste parent methods into every subclass
Hold collaborator objects and forward methods instead of inheriting many parents
questions.explanation: Delegation/composition provides reuse without multiple-implementation inheritance.

questions.stem: Can equals be consistent with inheritance when adding fields in a subclass? Classic pitfall?
options: Comparing getClass() vs instanceof leads to LSP/symmetry tensions; prefer composition or careful design
Always use instanceof and ignore symmetry concerns
Subclasses must not define any new fields ever
hashCode should ignore subclass fields always
questions.explanation: Extending equality across inheritance hierarchies easily breaks symmetry or LSP.

questions.stem: Circular dependency between two concrete classes — senior remediation?
options: Make both classes inherit from each other
Extract an interface, invert one dependency, or introduce an event/mediator
Delete equals and hashCode from both types
Mark both classes final and public only
questions.explanation: Break cycles with abstractions or by pushing interaction to a third collaborator.

questions.stem: Concurrency: publishing a mutable object before construction finishes — hazard?
options: Constructors are atomic across all JVMs for all fields always
Immutability makes escape analysis illegal
Other threads may see a partially initialized object (this escape)
final fields can never be visible to other threads
questions.explanation: Leaking this from a constructor can expose uninitialized state to other threads.

questions.stem: Visitor pattern versus adding methods to a sealed hierarchy — when is Visitor justified?
options: When you want to avoid compile-time exhaustiveness
Whenever you have exactly two subclasses
Only for immutable strings
When operations over a stable type set evolve often and you want double dispatch
questions.explanation: Visitor externalizes operations; modern sealed types + switches are often simpler.

questions.stem: Binary compatibility when evolving a public abstract class — senior caution?
options: Adding abstract methods breaks existing subclasses; prefer interfaces with defaults carefully
Any change to an abstract class is always binary-compatible
Abstract classes cannot be in public APIs
Removing methods never affects subclasses at link time
questions.explanation: Published abstract classes are hard to evolve; new abstract methods break implementors.

questions.stem: Object pools for mutable reusable instances — what correctness risk appears?
options: Pools make equals and hashCode unnecessary
Stale state leaking between borrowers unless reset is airtight
Pooled objects cannot implement interfaces
GC is disabled permanently when pooling
questions.explanation: Reuse requires rigorous cleanup; leftover mutable state causes subtle bugs.

questions.stem: Hexagonal architecture + OOP: what does a port/adapter boundary protect?
options: Controllers may access SQL drivers directly by design
All domain classes must extend JpaRepository
Domain model stays free of framework/UI/DB details behind interfaces
Adapters must never implement interfaces
questions.explanation: Ports are abstractions; adapters plug infrastructure without polluting the core.

questions.stem: Record / data class as a value object: what still needs senior judgment?
options: Records cannot implement interfaces in Java
Records automatically enforce all business rules
equals is unsafe on records and must be deleted
Validate invariants; avoid exposing mutable nested structures; define domain behavior if needed
questions.explanation: Concise value types still need validation and careful handling of nested mutability.

questions.stem: Multiple inheritance of state (C++): what classic problem do languages avoid with single inheritance + interfaces?
options: Diamond problem of duplicate base state and ambiguous method resolution
Inability to express polymorphism at all
Loss of encapsulation in private fields
Mandatory use of garbage collection
questions.explanation: Diamonds duplicate subobjects/state; Java-like models allow many interfaces, one class parent.

questions.stem: Designing for testability without over-mocking: which OOP practice helps most?
options: Make every method static for easier stubbing
Depend on narrow interfaces; keep pure domain logic free of static singletons and IO
Use only concrete final classes with private constructors everywhere
Put all logic in getters and setters
questions.explanation: Thin seams and pure core logic let tests stay fast without mocking the world.
